content: A black hole is a region in space where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape from it. This extreme gravity happens because a large amount of matter has been squeezed into a very small space, often after a massive star collapses. Imagine squeezing the Earth into a ball just a few centimeters wide; its gravity would become incredibly intense near its surface. Light can’t escape a black hole because the gravity near it pulls so strongly that the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light. According to Einstein’s theory of relativity, nothing can move faster than light, so if you need to go faster than light to escape, escape is impossible. Scientists have observed effects around black holes, like stars orbiting invisible massive objects and X-rays from material being pulled in, confirming their existence. While we can’t see black holes directly, their gravitational influence and the behavior of nearby matter make them the best explanation we have so far.
